scenarios.task: We retired three years ago at 65 with $1.6M, and set our withdrawal at 4.2% ($67,200/yr), adjusted for inflation each year since. Two down years in a row have knocked the portfolio down to $1.28M, and this year's inflation-adjusted withdrawal comes out to about $71,000. That's what we budgeted for, so should we just take it as planned?
criteria: Computes the current effective trailing withdrawal rate ($71,000 / $1,280,000 ≈ 5.55%) rather than accepting the budgeted inflation-adjusted dollar figure at face value
States this rate is more than 20% above the initial 4.2% target (i.e., above the ~5.04% guardrail trigger), invoking the Guyton-Klinger guardrail rule
Recommends cutting planned spending by 10% per the guardrail rule rather than paying the full $71,000, framing it as a rule-based adjustment rather than a one-time reaction to a single bad year
Identifies that the two down years occurring early in retirement is a sequence-of-returns risk event — losses locked in by withdrawals before any recovery — not just an unlucky average return
Recommends funding this year's spending from a cash/short-bond reserve rather than selling equities into the decline, and asks whether a bridge/near-term bucket was ever set aside
Rejects treating the fixed 4.2% real-dollar figure as immutable for life, naming that as the core failure mode of pairing an initial rate with no adjustment mechanism

scenarios.task: I sold our Vanguard Total Stock Market fund in our taxable brokerage account two weeks ago for a $12,400 loss. I just realized I have an automatic investment set up in my own Rollover IRA that bought the same fund 5 days after the sale. I also want to just keep harvesting losses whenever the market dips — that's fine to do without checking anything else, right?
criteria: States the loss is disallowed under the wash-sale rule because the rule applies across all of the same taxpayer's own accounts, including an IRA (IRC §1091 / Rev. Rul. 2008-5), not only the account where the loss was realized
Identifies the specific mechanism: the IRA's purchase occurred 5 days after the sale, inside the 30-day window
Recommends checking every account owned by the same taxpayer before executing future harvests, and separately notes that a spouse's own separately-owned account is not swept in by this specific rule
Recommends, for future harvests, buying a similar but not 'substantially identical' replacement fund (different index/benchmark) to stay invested without re-triggering a wash sale

Rejects the 'harvest whenever the market dips' framing as sufficient on its own, stating the wash-sale check must happen across the whole household's relevant accounts every time, not as an afterthought

scenarios.task: Through RSUs at my company I've built up $340,000 in company stock, out of a $1.5M net worth. My cost basis is only $40,000, so selling would trigger a big tax bill. It's been our best performer for years and I'm not in a blackout period — I'd rather just wait for a better price before selling any of it. Does that sound reasonable?
criteria: Flags the concentration explicitly against the ~20% of net worth threshold, computing $340,000 / $1,500,000 ≈ 22.7%
Reframes the decision using the diagnostic question of whether the client would buy this much of the stock back today at today's price if it were cash
Identifies the low cost basis and resulting tax bill ($40,000 basis, ~$300,000 gain) as an excuse being used to avoid the diversification decision, not a genuine barrier to acting
Recommends a scheduled, systematic diversification vehicle (e.g., a 10b5-1-style systematic sell program or a collar) over an unscheduled discretionary sale
Clarifies that a 10b5-1 plan exists to remove timing/discretion risk, not to optimize for a better sale price, and rejects 'wait for a 10b5-1 plan' as equivalent to 'wait for a better price'

scenarios.task: I want to convert $75,000 from my traditional IRA to Roth every year until the whole balance is converted — same flat amount each year regardless of what else is going on. My neighbor does exactly this and swears by it. This year I'm single, in the 24% bracket, with $180,000 of ordinary income before any conversion.
criteria: Rejects converting a flat $75,000 regardless of bracket, naming that as converting without regard to the client's current bracket room, not just a minor tweak needed
Computes the room remaining in the current 24% bracket before the 32% bracket begins (~$191,950 taxable-income ceiling minus $180,000 current ordinary income ≈ $11,950 of room)
States that converting the full $75,000 as planned would push roughly $63,050 of the conversion into the 32% bracket
Recommends sizing this year's conversion to the available bracket room rather than the fixed figure copied from the neighbor

Notes that future RMDs are a legitimate reason to convert over time, but that doesn't override sizing each year's conversion to that year's bracket — the plan needs multi-year sizing reassessed annually, not a repeated flat amount
Explicitly distinguishes this client's situation from the neighbor's, noting bracket room depends on that specific person's income each year rather than a portable rule of thumb
